target.name: (-)-flueggenine A
target.motivation: Securinega alkaloids are known for their unique structures and neuroplasticity-inducing potential, making them promising candidates for treating neurodegenerative diseases. The synthesis of flueggenine A, a dimeric securinega alkaloid, is crucial to complete the conjugation network of RC-based oligomeric securinega alkaloids.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: accelerated RC reaction strategy for (-)-flueggenine C
Stille cross-coupling and stereoselective conjugate reduction for flueggenine D

success_criteria.free_text: Total synthesis of (-)-flueggenine A and (-)-15'-epi-flueggenine D, achieving desired reactivity and stereoselectivity in the key reductive Heck dimerization step, and matching spectral data with natural product.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C(14)-C(15') bond (type A connection) and C(12)-C(15') bond (type B connection)
key_disconnections.reasoning: This disconnection forms the core dimeric structure. The stereochemical outcome was predicted to be influenced by the conformation of the enone coupling partner. A silyl-tethered enone was designed to enforce a specific conformation for desired stereoselectivity.

alternatives_considered.strategy: Stille cross-coupling and stereoselective conjugate reduction
alternatives_considered.rejected_because: These dimerization strategies could not be applied to the synthesis of flueggenine A or the 15'-epimer of flueggenine D.

key_disconnections.reasoning: A common method for forming butenolide rings.
A standard method for forming the pyrrolidine rings.
These reactions provide the necessary iodide coupling partners for the reductive Heck reaction.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The synthesis aimed to address the challenge of forming specific dimeric connections (types A and B) not accessible by previous methods. The central hypothesis was that controlling the conformation of the enone coupling partner in a reductive Heck reaction would enable the desired stereoselectivity. This led to the design of a silyl-tethered enone to enforce the correct conformation.

thought.background: Securinega alkaloids are known for their unique structures and neuroplasticity-inducing potential. Previous syntheses by our group and others have focused on monomeric and some dimeric securinega alkaloids, including flueggenine C and D.
thought.gap: The synthesis of flueggenine A and 15'-epi-flueggenine D, which represent type A and B connections, respectively, has remained challenging and is needed to complete the conjugation network of RC-based oligomeric securinega alkaloids.
thought.decision: To complete the synthetic access to RC-based oligomeric securinega alkaloids, we need to develop a new dimerization strategy that enables types A and B connections for the total synthesis of (-)-flueggenine A (6) and (-)-15'-epi-flueggenine D (7).

evidence.text_span: Despite signicant advancement in the total synthesis of monomeric securinega alkaloids,3,4 the synthesis of dimeric securinega alkaloids has been more challenging with fewer successful cases.18–23 Our group has been interested in the total synthesis of RC-based high-order securinega alkaloids.24,25 In 2017, we reported the total synthesis of $( - ) \cdot$ -ueggenine C (8) via an accelerated RC reaction strategy.26 In 2020, our group described the total synthesis of ueggenine D (9) enabled by a dimerization strategy involving a Stille cross-coupling reaction and a stereoselective conjugate reduction.27 However, these dimerization strategies could not be applied to the synthesis of ueggenine A (6) or the ${  oldsymbol { 1 5 ^ { \prime } } }$ -epimer of ueggenine D, the missing pieces needed to complete the conjugation network of RC-based oligomeric securinega alkaloids.28 Herein, we describe a new dimerization strategy that enables types A and B connections for the total synthesis of $( - )$ -ueggenine A (6) and $( - ) { - } 1 5 ^ { \prime }$ -epi-ueggenine D (7).

thought.background: The target molecules are (-)-flueggenine A (6) and (-)-15'-epi-flueggenine D (7).
thought.gap: How to form the dimeric connections (types A and B) with desired stereoselectivity.
thought.decision: To assemble the B-rings via intramolecular N-alkylation and butenolide D-rings via intramolecular Horner–Wadsworth–Emmons (HWE) reactions. The key dimerization step will be a reductive Heck reaction. Precursors 10 and 13 will be derived from reductive Heck reactions between alkenyl iodides (11 or 14) and enone 12. Iodides 11 and 14 will be accessed from enone 12 via alpha-iodination and iodo-Meyer–Schuster reaction, respectively. Stereochemical outcome of the reductive Heck reaction will be influenced by enone 12 conformation, aiming for (R)-configuration at the connection junction.

evidence.text_span:
Retrosynthetic analysis of $( - ) \cdot$ -ueggenine A (6) and $( - ) { - } 1 5 ^ { \prime } .$ -epi-ueggenine D (7) is presented in Scheme 1A. We envisioned to assemble the B-rings present in ueggenine A (6) and $1 5 ^ { \prime }$ -epi-ueggenine D (7) via parallel intramolecular $N \cdot$ -alkylation reactions. For the construction of the butenolide D-rings, intramolecular Horner–Wadsworth–Emmons (HWE) reactions would be employed. We planned to apply a reductive Heck reaction for the key dimerization. While ueggenine A precursor 10 would be derived from a reductive Heck reaction between alkenyl iodide 11 and enone 12, epi-ueggenine D precursor 13 was designed to be accessed from a reductive Heck reaction between iodide 14 and the common enone 12. Iodides 11 and 14 were planned to be accessed from common enone 12 via an $\pmb { \alpha }$ -iodination reaction and an iodo-Meyer–Schuster reaction, respectively.
We predicted that the stereochemical outcome of the reductive Heck reaction would be inuenced by the conformation of enone 12. Enone 12 would adopt either conformation A or B (Scheme 1B). According to the Fürst–Plattner rule,29 when compound 12 assumes conformation A, the alkenylpalladium species approaches from the top face to avoid an unfavorable twist boat-like transition state, resulting in product 15 with the desired (R)-conguration at the connection junction. On the other hand, in conformation B, the alkenylpalladium intermediate would approach from the bottom face to yield compound
16 with an (S)-conguration at the connecting carbon. For the latter case, we anticipated that the protected allylic hydroxyl moiety may hinder the approach of the organopalladium species. Taking into consideration the sterically bulky nature of the Boc-protected pyrrolidine moiety, we initially anticipated that conformation A of enone 12 would be favored over conformation B.

thought.background: The reductive Heck dimerization strategy is envisioned as the key step. Initial plausibility test using model compounds.
thought.gap: Confirm if the reductive Heck reaction is feasible and if stereoselectivity can be achieved.
thought.decision: To test the reductive Heck reaction with alkenyl iodide 17 and cyclohexenone (18) as a model system, expecting to obtain product 19 with good yield and diastereoselectivity.

outcome.observation: The desired reductive Heck product 19 was obtained in 76% yield as a 22:1 mixture of diastereomers, which was encouraging.

evidence.text_span: To test the plausibility of the reductive Heck dimerization strategy, we initially attempted the reaction with alkenyl iodide 17 and cyclohexenone (18). Pleasantly, when iodide 17 and cyclohexenone (18) were heated in the presence of $5 ~ \mathrm { m o l\% }$ of palladium(II) triuoroacetate $\left( \mathrm { P d } \left( \mathrm { T F A } ight) _ { 2 } ight)$ and diisopropylethylamine (DIPEA) in $N \cdot$ -methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP),30 reductive Heck reaction product 19 was obtained in $7 6 \%$ yield as a $2 2 : 1$ mixture of diastereomers (Scheme 2). Encouraged by these

thought.background: The model reductive Heck reaction was successful. Now, apply the reaction to the actual enone coupling partner.
thought.gap: The reaction might not translate directly to the more complex enone 20.
thought.decision: To attempt the reductive Heck reaction between iodide 17 and enone 20, expecting to form the conjugated product 21.

outcome.observation: The desired conjugated product 21 was not formed, even after extensive experimentation. Deiodination of compound 17 was the major undesired pathway, yielding enone 20.

evidence.text_span: results, we next attempted the reductive Heck reaction between iodide 17 and enone 20. However, even aer extensive experimentation, the desired conjugated product 21 was not formed (Scheme 2). Deiodination of compound 17 was the major undesired pathway, yielding enone 20. This suggests that while the oxidative addition of the palladium catalyst to alkenyl iodide 17 was occurring, the migratory insertion of the resulting organopalladium intermediate into enone 20 was hindered.

thought.background: The reductive Heck reaction with enone 20 failed, indicating a problem with reactivity or stereoselectivity.
thought.gap: Understand why enone 20 did not react as expected and what its preferred conformation is.
thought.decision: To perform single-crystal X-ray diffraction (SCXD) analysis of enone 20 to determine its conformation and solution phase DFT-calculations to corroborate the conformational preference.

outcome.observation: SCXD data revealed that compound 20 adopts a conformation where the Boc-protected pyrrolidine moiety is positioned pseudo-axially, while the two silyl ether groups are arranged pseudo-equatorially. Solution phase DFT-calculations corroborated this conformational preference. In this conformation, the approach of the alkenylpalladium species from the top face would be inhibited by steric hindrance, and the approach from the bottom face is also hindered by the silylether group.

evidence.text_span: Further insights into the lack of the desired reactivity were gained through single-crystal X-ray diffraction (SCXD) analysis of enone 20 (CCDC number of compound 20: 2385997). Contrary to our initial predictions, the SCXD data revealed that compound 20 adopts a conformation where the Boc-protected pyrrolidine moiety is positioned pseudo-axially, while the two silyl ether groups are arranged pseudo-equatorially (Scheme 3). Solution phase DFT-calculations of enone 20 also corroborated this conformational preference (see the ESI for details†). In this conformation, the approach of the alkenylpalladium species from the top face would be inhibited by steric hindrance caused by the bulky Boc-protected pyrrolidine moiety and the unfavored twist boat-like transition state. The absence of the reductive Heck product with an (S)-conguration at the connection junction suggests that the approach of the organopalladium intermediate from the bottom face is also hindered, presumably due to the presence of the silylether group.

thought.background: Enone 20's conformation prevents the desired reductive Heck reaction. A ring flip is needed to achieve the correct reactivity and stereoselectivity.
thought.gap: How to induce a ring flip in the enone conformation to allow for the desired approach of the alkenylpalladium species.
thought.decision: To design a silyl-tethered enone (compound 22) by tethering the two hydroxyl groups in compound 20. This tethering is expected to enforce an axial positioning of the hydroxyls, promoting a ring flip where the Boc-protected pyrrolidine group is positioned pseudo-equatorially. This should allow an unhindered approach from the top face, consistent with the Fürst–Plattner rule, to yield the desired type A and B connections.

outcome.observation: The design of silyl-tethered enone 22 was proposed to promote the desired ring-flipped conformation. SCXD analysis of the alpha-iodinated derivative of 22 (compound 23) confirmed that the silyl-tethered enone adopts the desired ring-flipped conformation.

evidence.text_span:
These observations made it clear that we needed to induce a ring ip in the conformation of the reductive Heck acceptor enone to achieve the desired reactivity with the correct stereochemistry. Historically, there have been elegant examples where an energetically less favorable conformation was induced through transitory covalent bond formation to achieve the desired reactivity and selectivity.31–35 Inspired by these precedents, we envisioned tethering the two hydroxyl groups in compound 20 to enforce their axial positioning. To accomplish this, we designed silyl-tethered enone compound 22 to promote a ring ip. We anticipated that this silyl-tethered compound would adopt a conformation in which the Boc-protected pyrrolidine group is positioned pseudo-equatorially (Scheme 3). Indeed, SCXD analysis of the $\pmb { \alpha }$ -iodinated derivative of 22
conrmed that the silyl-tethered enone adopts the desired ring-ipped conformation (CCDC number of compound 23: 2385995). With this newly designed silyl-tethered reductive Heck acceptor enone 22, we predicted an unhindered approach of the alkenylpalladium species from the top face of it, complying with a Fürst–Plattner rule, to yield the desired types A and B connections present in ueggenine A (6) and ${  oldsymbol { 1 5 ^ { \prime } } } .$ -epi-ueggenine D (7).

thought.background: The starting material is gamma-hydroxyenone 24. The goal is to prepare the alpha-iodoenone 17 for the reductive Heck reaction.
thought.gap: Need to selectively protect the hydroxyl group and then perform alpha-iodination.
thought.decision: To protect the hydroxyl group of 24 with TBS, followed by alpha-iodination of the enone moiety.

outcome.observation: TBS protection of the hydroxyl group yielded 20 in 91%. Subsequent alpha-iodination of 20 with iodine in pyridine and chloroform co-solvent yielded 17 in 94%.

evidence.text_span: The synthesis of reductive Heck reaction coupling partners commenced with our previously accessed $ oldsymbol { \Upsilon }$ -hydroxyenone compound 24. 26 For the synthesis of $\pmb { \alpha }$ -iodoenone 17, ghydroxyenone 24 was subjected to a two-step protocol involving TBS protection of the hydroxyl group $( 9 1 \%$ yield) and $\pmb { \alpha }$ -iodination of the enone moiety with iodine in pyridine and chloroform co-solvent $9 4 \%$ yield).

thought.background: The silyl-tethered enone 22 is crucial for the desired stereoselectivity in the reductive Heck reaction.
thought.decision: To remove the TMS group of 24 to yield diol 25, then react 25 with dichlorodiisopropylsilane to form the silyl-tethered enone 22.

outcome.observation: Removing the TMS group of 24 yielded diol 25 in >99% yield. Diol 25 reacted with dichlorodiisopropylsilane to produce the key silyl tethered enone 22 in 94% yield.

evidence.text_span: The synthesis of silyl tethered enone 22 was achieved by rst removing the TMS group of 24 in the presence of triethylamine trihydrouoride to yield diol 25 in ${  f > 9 9 \% }$ yield (Scheme 4). Diol 25 was subsequently allowed to react with dichlorodiisopropylsilane in the presence of DIPEA to produce the key silyl tethered enone 22 in $9 4 \%$ yield.

thought.background: Another coupling partner, iodobutenolide 28, is needed for the synthesis of 15'-epi-flueggenine D.
thought.gap: Need to synthesize iodobutenolide 28 from diol 25.
thought.decision: To selectively protect the secondary allylic hydroxyl group in diol 25, then react the resulting ketone 26 with lithium phenoxyacetylide (generated from phenoxy dichloroethene 27) to form a propargylic alcohol intermediate, followed by reaction with NIS to yield iodobutenolide 28 via iodo-Meyer–Schuster rearrangement.

outcome.observation: Selective TBS protection of diol 25 yielded ketone 26 in 92%. Reaction of 26 with lithium phenoxyacetylide and subsequent reaction with NIS yielded iodobutenolide 28 in 47% over two steps. The structure of 28 was corroborated by SCXD analysis.

evidence.text_span: The synthesis of iodobutenolide compound 28 involved selective TBS protection of the secondary allylic hydroxyl group in diol 25. The resulting ketone compound 26 was reacted with lithium phenoxyacetylide generated in situ from phenoxy dichloroethene 27 to yield the propargylic alcohol intermediate. This 1,2-addition product was subsequently allowed to react with $N -$ iodosuccinimide (NIS) to yield iodobutenolide 28 via a presumed iodo-Meyer–Schuster rearrangement in $4 7 \%$ yield over two steps.27,36,37 The structure of iodide 28 was corroborated by SCXD analysis (CCDC number of compound 28: 2385999).

thought.background: Iodoenone 17 and silyl-tethered enone 22 are prepared. The silyl-tethered enone 22 is designed to promote the desired stereoselectivity.
thought.gap: Execute the key reductive Heck reaction to form the type A connection.
thought.decision: To perform the reductive Heck reaction between iodoenone 17 and silyl-tethered enone 22 in the presence of Pd(TFA)2 and DIPEA, expecting to obtain product 29 with (R)-configuration at the connection junction.

outcome.observation: The desired reductive Heck product 29 with the (R)-configuration at the connection junction (type A connection) was obtained in 70% yield, consistent with the stereochemical model.

evidence.text_span: With iodoenone 17 and silyl tethered enone 22 in hand, we executed the key reductive Heck reaction in the presence of $\mathbf { P d } ( \mathbf { TFA } ) _ { 2 }$ and DIPEA. To our utmost delight, the desired reductive Heck product 29 with the (R)-conguration at the connection junction (type A connection) was obtained in $7 0 \%$ yield consistent with our stereochemical model (Schemes 3 and 4).

thought.background: Reductive Heck product 29 is formed. The silyl protecting groups need to be removed and the hydroxyl groups acetylated for further steps.
thought.gap: Need to perform global desilylation and acetylation.
thought.decision: To subject compound 29 to HF-pyridine-mediated global desilylation, followed by acetylation of the resulting product to afford diacetate 30.

outcome.observation: Global desilylation and subsequent acetylation afforded diacetate 30 in 88% yield over two steps.

evidence.text_span: HF$pyridine-mediated global desilylation of compound 29 and subsequent acetylation of the resulting product afforded diacetate 30 in $8 8 \%$ yield over two steps.

thought.background: The silyl-tethered enone 22 and iodobutenolide 28 are prepared.
thought.gap: Execute the key reductive Heck reaction to form the type B connection for 15'-epi-flueggenine D.
thought.decision: To react iodobutenolide 28 and silyl-tethered enone 22 in the presence of Pd(OAc)2 and DIPEA, expecting to furnish conjugated product 31 with type B connectivity.

outcome.observation: Iodobutenolide 28 and silyl-tethered enone 22 successfully furnished conjugated product 31 with type B connectivity in 35% yield.

evidence.text_span: To our pleasure, iodobutenolide 28 and silyl tethered enone 22 successfully furnished conjugated product 31 with type B connectivity in the presence of $\mathbf { P d } ( \mathbf { O A c } ) _ { 2 }$ and DIPEA in $3 5 \%$ yield.

thought.background: Reductive Heck product 31 is formed. Similar to compound 29, the silyl protecting groups need to be removed and the hydroxyl groups acetylated.
thought.gap: Need to perform global desilylation and acetylation for compound 31.
thought.decision: To subject silyl ether 31 to the same desilylation and acetylation protocol used for compound 29 to obtain acetylated compound 32.

outcome.observation: Acetylated compound 32 was obtained in 93% yield over two steps. The structure of compound 32 was unambiguously confirmed by SCXD analysis.

evidence.text_span: When silyl ether 31 was subjected to the aforementioned desilylation and acetylation protocol, acetylated compound 32 was obtained in $9 3 \%$ yield over two steps. The structure of compound 32 was unambiguously conformed by its SCXD analysis (Scheme 5, CCDC number of compound 32: 2386004).

thought.background: Diacetate 30 is prepared. The final steps involve forming the butenolide moiety and intramolecular N-alkylation to construct the octacyclic framework of (-)-flueggenine A (6).
thought.gap: Need to implement the HWE reaction and N-alkylation sequence.
thought.decision: To treat diol 30 with diethylphosphonoacetic acid and DCC to yield the HWE precursor, followed by reaction with DBU to produce butenolide 33. Then, methanolysis of 33 to diol 34, mesylation of the diol, TFA-mediated Boc-deprotection, and base treatment to afford (-)-flueggenine A (6).

outcome.observation: Butenolide compound 33 was produced in 46% yield over two steps. Methanolysis of 33 yielded diol 34 in 94%. Mesylation, Boc-deprotection, and base treatment afforded (-)-flueggenine A (6) in 63% yield over 3 steps. Spectral data matched the natural product.

evidence.text_span:
The end-game of the synthetic campaign toward $( - ) \cdot$ -ueggenine A (6) involved parallel installation of the butenolide moiety and intramolecular $N \cdot$ -alkylation to construct the octacyclic framework. The butenolide moiety was introduced by treating diol 30 with diethylphosphonoacetic acid in the presence of $^ { N , N ^ { \prime } }$ -dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), yielding the HWE precursor. Subsequent reaction of the resulting ester with 1,8-
diazabicyclo(5.4.0)undec-7-ene (DBU) produced the butenolide compound 33 in $4 6 \%$ yield over two steps (Scheme 5). For the Nalkylation, acetate 33 was subjected to methanolysis, yielding diol 34 in $9 4 \%$ yield. The diol moiety was then activated by mesylation. Triuoroacetic acid (TFA)-mediated Bocdeprotection of the resulting carbamate intermediate, followed by base treatment of the secondary amine, afforded the rst synthetic sample of $( - ) \cdot$ -ueggenine A (6) in $6 3 \%$ yield over 3 steps. The spectral data of the synthetic compound matched those of the natural product.15

thought.background: Acetylated compound 32 is prepared. The final steps involve forming the butenolide moiety and intramolecular N-alkylation to construct the octacyclic framework of 15'-epi-flueggenine D (7).
thought.gap: Need to apply the same end-game protocol as for flueggenine A.
thought.decision: To apply the same end-game protocol (HWE reaction, methanolysis, mesylation, Boc-deprotection, and base treatment) to alcohol 32 to synthesize 15'-epi-flueggenine D (7).

outcome.observation: Applying the same end-game protocol to alcohol 32 resulted in the successful synthesis of 15'-epi-flueggenine D (7) in analogous efficiency. The structure of 15'-epi-flueggenine D (7) was unequivocally corroborated by its SCXD analysis.

evidence.text_span: To our delight, applying the same end-game protocol to alcohol 32 resulted in the successful synthesis of ${  f 1 5 ^ { \prime } }$ -epi-ueggenine D (7) in analogous efficiency compared to the route to $\left( - ight)$ -ueggenine A (6). The structure of ${  f 1 5 ^ { \prime } }$ -epi-ueggenine D (7) was unequivocally corroborated by its SCXD analysis (CCDC number of compound 7: 2387436).

validation.significance: The total synthesis of (-)-flueggenine A and (-)-15'-epi-flueggenine D was achieved, completing the synthetic access to RC-based oligomeric securinega alkaloids. The key innovation was the design and use of a silyl-tethered reductive Heck acceptor enone, which enabled the desired reactivity and stereoselectivity for type A and B linkages. This new dimerization strategy, combined with existing methods for type C and D connections, provides a comprehensive synthetic solution for this important class of natural products.

source.title: Total synthesis of (-)-flueggenine A and (-)-15'-epi-flueggenine D.
source.authors: Seo SM, Kim D, Kim T, Han S.
